coredata.dc:title: Designing boron-doped carbon dot-functionalized COFs for fluorescence screening and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry detection of toxins
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yin-Hai
Xu, Fei
Zhao, Wei-Long
Tang, Xiao-Fan
Liu, Feng
Bo, Chun-Miao
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, mushroom poisoning has been one of the most important factors of food poisoning in China, timely identification of the toxins contained in mushrooms is crucial for the treatment of patients. In this study, boric acid carbon dots (BA-CDs) can undergo specific boron affinity reactions with amatoxins toxins containing o-dihydroxyl groups by means of boric acid groups. Functional covalent organic framework (COF) and BA-CDs were combined to design a adsorbent with boric acid group (COF@VBC@BA-CDs) was designed to meet the requirements of both fluorescent and pretreated materials for amatoxins. The mushrooms and urine samples were rapid screening using fluorescence detection, and then, for positive samples, the target analytes on the COF@VBC@BA-CDs are collected and eluted for next liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) detection. According to the fluorescence characteristics of COF@VBC@BA-CDs, the fluorescence quenching intensity was linearly correlated with the concentration (2–200 μg/L) and the detection limit was 1.2 μg/L. Meanwhile, the detection limit of LC-MS/MS was 0.5 μg/kg for musroom and 0.2 μg/L for urine, as well as the recovery rate was 72.7–110.1%. This noval method meets the methodological requirements and can be used for actual sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic framework
Amatoxins
Fluorescence detection
Liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22364001 and 22064013), Nature Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2023AAC03259), Science and Technology Support Program of Yinchuan, Ningxia (No. 2024SF001), Youth Talent Cultivation Project of North Minzu University (No. 2021KYQD30), University level key research project of North Minzu University (No. 2023ZRLG19).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis of COF@VBC@BA-CDs and (B) schematic diagram of detection process.
SEM images of (A) COF, (C) COF@VBC@BA-CDs at 8000 x, (B) COF, (D) COF@VBC@BA-CDs at 20 000 x, and (E) Elemental mapping images of C, N, O, and S element of COF.
Total ion chromatograms (TIC) of (A) mushroom sample and (B) urine sample after treated with COF@VBC@BA-CDs.
Linear range, regression equation, correlation coefficient, LOD and LOQ of amatoxins and phalloidins.
The intra-day recovery rate, RSD, inter-day recovery rate, and RSD of mushroom and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Designing boron-doped carbon dot-functionalized COFs for fluorescence screening and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry detection of toxins

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 204 Wenchang North Street, Xixia District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, mushroom poisoning has been one of the most important factors of food poisoning in China, timely identification of the toxins contained in mushrooms is crucial for the treatment of patients. In this study, boric acid carbon dots (BA-CDs) can undergo specific boron affinity reactions with amatoxins toxins containing o-dihydroxyl groups by means of boric acid groups. Functional covalent organic framework (COF) and BA-CDs were combined to design a adsorbent with boric acid group (COF@VBC@BA-CDs) was designed to meet the requirements of both fluorescent and pretreated materials for amatoxins. The mushrooms and urine samples were rapid screening using fluorescence detection, and then, for positive samples, the target analytes on the COF@VBC@BA-CDs are collected and eluted for next liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) detection. According to the fluorescence characteristics of COF@VBC@BA-CDs, the fluorescence quenching intensity was linearly correlated with the concentration (2–200 μg/L) and the detection limit was 1.2 μg/L. Meanwhile, the detection limit of LC-MS/MS was 0.5 μg/kg for musroom and 0.2 μg/L for urine, as well as the recovery rate was 72.7–110.1%. This noval method meets the methodological requirements and can be used for actual sample analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mushroom poisoning is one of the most important food poisoning factors in China. According to the statistics of the Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention, from 2019 to 2023, a total of 2266 mushroom poisoning incidents occurred in China, resulting in 6046 patients and 111 deaths, with a mortality rate of 1.84 % [1–4]. In all cases of mushroom poisoning deaths, ingestion of some mushrooms containing amatoxins and phallotoxins accounted for more than 90% of the total incidents [5]. In nature, amatoxins and phallotoxins are highly toxic cyclic peptides found in some wild mushrooms such as Amanita, Galerina, and Lepiota, which can cause to severe liver and kidney damage [6,7]. In addition, they have also been found in Pseudosperma umbrinellum [8]. These mushroom toxins are the main substances responsible for poisoning, so the initial identification of the type of toxin ingested by the patient is crucial for clinicians to provide targeted treatment to the poisoned person.

At present, there are more analysis methods in human biological samples of mushroom toxins after poisoning, mainly including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [9–12]. Among them, the advantages of ELISA is mainly that the operation is relatively simple, does not require complex equipment, has a low cost, and can be carried out with high throughput. However, it cannot provide accurate quantitative analysis of trace substances [13,14]. Compared with other methods, LC-MS/MS shows high selectivity and sensitivity, as well as sufficient qualitative and confirmatory capabilities. Therefore, it has been widely developed and applied in the analysis of amatoxins and phallotoxins [15]. However, LC-MS/MS requires a cumbersome pre-treatment process, which requires a lot of manpower and material resources in the face of a large sample base [16,17]. Therefore, it is urgent to solve the problem of heavy workload in the testing process. From the point of view of reducing the workload of inspectors, rapid sample screening without extraction and processing should be an effective method to simplify the toxin detection procedure when facing a large number of samples. Fluorescence detection has been widely used in various fields because of its excellent performance, simple operation, high selectivity and rapid analysis [18–20], and there are few reports on fluorescence detection of toxins. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a method that can simplify the workload and carry out accurate quantification for the detection of mushroom poisoning.

However, during LC-MS/MS analysis, it is often found that endogenous substances in biological samples coexist with target substances, resulting in matrix effects. Sample pretreatment has been shown to be the most effective method to reduce matrix effects, and sample preparation prior to instrumental analysis is essential [17,21]. Dispersive solid phase extraction (d-SPE) is applied as a sample preparation method for the extraction of organic and inorganic analytes from real samples with complex matrices [22]. Extensive applications of d-SPE are due to its high benefits, such as simplicity, high extraction efficiency, high preconcentration factor, ability be coupled with different instruments, and low cost [23,24]. Three essential steps in the sample preparation method, including concentration, separation, and clean-up steps, are also carried out at one-step with the d-SPE procedure. As a promising adsorbent, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have the characteristics of high order crystal structure, good chemical stability, large specific surface area, low density, good functionalization, etc. [25]. However, most COFs mainly rely on non-specific interactions to adsorb target substances. In order to achieve specific recognition of target substances, functional modification of COFs is an effective strategy [26,27]. Carbon dots (CDs) as a quasi-zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm, can be used for the detection of specific target substances by using its fluorescence characteristics, such as fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement and ratio fluorescence, due to its excellent light stability and good biocompatibility [28–31]. The binding of CDs to adsorbents has been extensively studied. However, there are few reports on the combination of COFs and CDs as adsorbents.

Herein, a simple method of simultaneous preparation of mercaptan functionalization COF@VBC@BA-CDs for selective extraction of amatoxins and phallotoxins is designed. In this strategy, the cleavage of disulfide bonds plays a dual role of mercaptan modification and mesoporous formation, and large-sized molecules such as polypeptides can not only enter the internal pores of the material, but also increase the hydrophobic force and boron affinity force between the adsorbent and the target material, so that it has higher adsorption performance. In the actual sample analysis process, the COF@VBC@BA-CDs can be used for fluorescence screening of the target substance from a large number of samples, and then the adsorbent in positive sample is used for d-SPE-LC-MS/MS. This study provides a noval idea for the detection of mushroom toxins.

Six amatoxins and phallotoxins standard substances of α-amanitin (α-AMA), β-amanitin (β-AMA), γ-amanitin (γ-AMA), phalloidin (POD), phallacidin (PCD) and phallisacin (PSC) (purity ≥ 95%) were provided from Alta Scientific Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 1,3,5-triformyl-phloroglycinol (Tp) and 4,4\'-(Ethane-1,2-diyl)dianiline (EDA), 4-Aminophenyl disulfide (DDS) as mixed monomers, glutathione (GSH) and 4-vinylbenzyl chloride (VBC) were provided from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin, China). Other materials and reagents are detailed in Supplementary Information.

The fluorescence detection (FLD) of the developed material was analyzed by the LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Company, Japan). The liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) consisted of a Shimadzu LC-20AD Prominence UFLC system (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) and an AB SCIEX 4000 QTRAP MS/MS system equipped with a turbo ion spray source employed in the electrospray ionization (ESI) interface (AB SCIEX, Foster City, USA). Other characterization and analysis instruments are displayed in Supplementary Information.

According to our previous study [27], the synthesis procedure was modified. The preparation of COF@VBC@BA-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 . Hybrid COFs containing disulfide bonds are synthesized by schiff base reaction using Tp and EDA and DDS as mixed monomers. GSH reduces the disulfide bond in the backbone COFs to form a sulfhydryl functional group, and the disulfide bond breaks resulting in a "defect structure", which increases the pore size, and photolithogenic click reaction with VBC. Finally, BA-CDs containing boric acid groups and amino groups undergo nucleophilic substitution with halogens. The specific procedure is described in the Supplementary Information.

Amatoxins and phallotoxins standard solution (15.0 mL) was prepared with the concentration of 2.0–1000.0 μg/L with pH = 9.0, and then 1.0 mg COF@VBC@BA-CDs was added. After shaking for 10.0 min, the adsorption concentration was determined using LC-MS/MS. According to the difference in the concentration of the target substance, its adsorption capacity (Q) was calculated according to Eq. (1) as follow:
(1)
Q =
(
C 0
−
C e
)
V / m
Where C 0 and C e (mg/L) are the concentration of analytes before and after adsorption, V (L) is the volume of solution, m (g) is the mass of adsorbent.

The Langmuir model (Eq. (2)) and Freundlich model (Eq. (3)) were used to fit the isothermal adsorption curve of COF@VBC@BA-CDs.
(2)
C e
/
Q e
=
C e
/
Q m
+ 1 /
K L
Q m
(3)
ln
Q e
= ln
C e
/ n + ln
K F

Where Q e is equilibrium adsorption capacity and Q m (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity, respectively. K L (L/mg) and K F (mg1-n Ln/g) are the equilibrium constants of Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively.

Pesudo-first-order (Eq. (4)) and pesudo-second-order (Eq. (5)) rate kinetic models were used to investigate whether the rate determination step was physical adsorption or chemical adsorption.
(4)
ln
(
Q e
−
Q t
)
= ln
Q e
−
K 1
t
(5)
t /
Q t
= 1 /
K 2
Q e
2
+ t /
Q e

Q t (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity at t (min), K 1 and K 2 (g/mg min) are pesudo-first-order and pesudo-second-order rate constants, respectively. The corresponding values can be obtained by plotting the line intercept and slope of ln (Q e-Q t) against t and t/Q t against t.

The 5.0 mL mixed test solution consisted of six amatoxins and phallotoxins as targets, RR, LR, ribavirin and bithrin as disruptors with each concentrations of 20.0 μg/L, and subsequently adjusted to the pH = 9.0. The test d-SPE including COF, COF@VBC and COF@VBC@BA-CDs were used for purification to evaluate selectivity. The eluents were blown to near-dry using nitrogen flow at 50 °C. Here after, the residue was redissolved with 1.0 mL of acetonitrile–water solution (15:85, v/v) and filtered using 0.2 μm nylon microporous membrane before LC-MS/MS. All tests were repeated three times (n = 3).

The concentration of six amatoxins and phallotoxins was 20.0 μg/L. Firstly, the influence of pH and material amount on FLD was considered. Then, for next d-SPE befor LC-MS/MS, the eluent ratio and eluent volume were optimized. When one of these conditions changed, the other conditions were fixed. The average values were calculated (n = 3).

For mushroom samples, 10.0 mg mushroom sample was placed in 2.0 mL methanol–water solution (30:70, v/v) under ultrasonic environment for 5 min. After centrifugation at 15,000 r/min for 2 min, a certain amount of supernatant was taken for both FLD and LC-MS/MS.

For urine sample, 15.0 mL urine sample was filtered with 0.2 μm Nylon multi-layer microporous membrane to remove solid impurities. A certain amount of urine sample was further used for both FLD and LC-MS/MS.

The 10.0 mg COF@VBC@BA-CDs material was placed in 2.5 mL deionized water, and the above 2.0 mL sample supernatant was added to ultrasonic dispersion. The pH was adjusted to 9.0, and fluorescence spectral analysis was performed at the excitation wavelength of 305 nm. In addition, 2.0 mL ultra-pure water replaced 2.0 mL sample extracts as the charge sample for FLD as a control group (fluorescence intensity was recorded as F0).

The above fluorescence screened sample solutions were centrifugated under 10,000 r/min at 5 min and then collected COF@VBC@BA-CDs materials. Hereafter, the impurities were washed with 5.0 mL water and the targets were eluted with 5.0 mL acetic acid/methanol/water solution (0.5:99.5:0, v/v/v), respectively. The eluent is blown dry with nitrogen, as well as 1.0 mL acetonitrile–water solution (15:85, v/v) was re-dissolved for LC-MS/MS detection.

In this work, the COF containing sulfhydryl group was first prepared and click reaction with VBC monomer was carried out through photoinitiation. Then, the halogen contained in VBC was nucleophilically substituted with amino group BA-CDs. The modification of carbon points can not only preliminarily screened the target substances using FLD, but also increased their adsorption properties as d-SPE adsorption material.

The surface morphologies of COF and COF@VBC@BA-CDs at different magnification were observed using SEM. The COF were dendritic and clustered to form a multi-branch network. The COF special structure was uniformly distributed with C, N, O, and S (Fig. 2 A, 2B). Among all the constituent elements, S was derived from the thiol-group formed by disulfide bond cleavage in the hybrid DDS (Fig. 2E). The image of COF@VBC@BA-CDs was similar to COF (Fig. 2C, 2D), indicating that COF does not change its shape and basic structure after modification with VBC and BA-CDs.

The pore size and pore volume of the developed materials were measured using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. S1). Thus, COF, COF@VBC and COF@VBC@BA-CDs specific surface areas were 95, 94 and 34 m2/g, as well as pore volumes were 0.41, 0.37 and 0.14 cm3/g, respectively. The decrease in pore volume may be due to partial photoinitiation of polymer into pores. In addition, the pore size distribution of COF was about 17.4 nm, and with the continuous modification of VBC and BA-CDs, the average pore size was reduced to 17.1 and 15.5 nm, respectively, indicating that for large molecules such as amatoxins and phallotoxins, which can physically entered the pores of the material.

The XPS spectra of COF showed C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and S 2p peak (163 eV), the result showed that the existence of -SH in COF and confirmed the feasibility of subsequent click reaction (Fig. S2A). By further chemical modification, the Cl element (199 eV) in VBC appeared in COF@VBC, while there was no corresponding peak in COF, proving the success of the click reaction. In addition, compared with COF@VBC, there was B 1s (168 eV) in COF@VBC@BA-CDs, and the content of Cl 1s was less, indicating that nucleophilic substitution occurred between BA-CDs and Cl, which can prove the successful synthesis of COF@VBC@BA-CDs.

The COF, COF@VBC and COF@VBC@BA-CDs functional groups were characterized using FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2B). The wide peaks at 3315 and 1513 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra COF were caused by N−H stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1292 and 1590 cm−1 were attributed to the tensile vibrations of C−N and C=C, respectively. The results showed that COF were formed by schiff base reaction. For COF@VBC, the peak value at 700 cm-1 was CCl bending vibration, which verified the completion of the click response in which VBC participated. For COF@VBC@BA-CDs, the peak value at 1020 cm−1 corresponded to the tensile vibration of B-O-H. The results showed that COF@VBC@BA-CDs was synthesized successfully.

In summary, the static adsorption capacities of COF@VBC@BA-CDs for target materials were 6.6–8.3 mg/g, respectively, which was consistent with Langmuir model and belonged to monolayer adsorption. The static adsorption capacity were 6.7–8.4 mg/g, which conformed to the pseudo-second-order model and was chemisorption. Details were shown in the Supplementary Information.

Beyond that, the adsorption capacities between toxins and COF@VBC@BA-CDs was evaluated by comparison of the reported adsorption materials (Table S3). The adsorption amounts using COF@VBC@BA-CDs (0.41 to 4.82 mg/g) were remarkably higher than imprinted silica microspheres (0.9 mg/g) [32], RP/PBA mixed-mode magnetic microspheres (0.58–0.67 mg/g) [33], Fe3O4/P4VPBA/PPy/GO composite materia (0.83–0.97 mg/g) [34], molecularly imprinted polymers (0.41–4.82 mg/g) [35,36], respectively. Meanwhile, 8.0 min adsorption time indicates that the adsorption rate is relatively fast using COF@VBC@BA-CDs. Hence, the designed functional materials not only possessed the adsorption property but also had the remarkably adsorption effect.

The selectivity of COF@VBC@BA-CDs was compared with COF and COF@VBC as controls (Fig. S5). The recoveries of COF and COF@VBC for RTC without benzene ring structure were 31.5% and 31.7%, respectively. The recoveries of bifenthrin with benzene ring structure were 66.0% and 74.7%, respectively. The recoveries of LR and RR were 72.0–72.7% and 81.7–81.8%, respectively. The recoveries of six target substance were 71.2–84.9%. The results showed that COF and COF@VBC could adsorb the compounds with benzene ring structure by hydrophobic force, and the adsorption recovery of the compounds without benzene ring structure (31.5–31.7%) was lower than benzene ring structure compounds (66.0–84.9%). However, the recoveries of COF@VBC@BA-CDs for RTC without benzene ring structure were 82.7%. The recoveries of bifenthrin with benzene ring structure were 75.3%. Microcystins were cyclic peptide structure but did not contain o-hydroxyl, therefore, recoveries of LR and RR were 83.8% and 84.8%, respectively. The recoveries of six amatoxins and phallotoxins were 83.7–98.4%. Because COF@VBC@BA-CDs had both phenylboric acid and benzene ring structure, the benzene ring structure with o-hydroxyl structure can be adsorbed by boric acid affinity and other forces. Compared with compounds without o-hydroxyl structure, the recovery rate of substances containing o-dihydroxyl was higher. It can be seen that COF@VBC@BA-CDs had a good selectivity for target substances.

In summary, the pH of the sample solution was 9.0, the material dosage was 10 mg, the volume of eluent acetic acid/methanol/water solution (0.5:99.5:0, v/v/v) was 5.0 mL (Fig. S7). Under these optimal conditions, COF@VBC@BA-CDs had the best adsorption for targets. The detailed process was shown in the Supplementary Information.

In summary, the designed COF@VBC@BA-CDs exhibit both fluorescence property and enrichment capabilities for amatoxins and phallotoxins. The specific operation process is COF@VBC@BA-CDs are first used as fluorescence probes for preliminary FLD screening in actual samples. And then, the positive samples are confirmed and quantitatively analyzed using d-SPE-LC-MS/MS.

All the treated samples were mixed with COF@VBC@BA-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 305 nm for FLD (Fig. S6A). F0 was simply detected by the initial COF@VBC@BA-CDs probe and F combined with the probe and the mushroom sample. The calculated F/F0 was used to evaluate the sample for the presence of toxins. According to the fluorescence method we established (Fig. S6B), the lowest detectable concentration of the toxin was 1.20 μg/L, corresponding to 0.38 of F/F0. Therefore, in the preliminary FLD screening, F/F0 of 0.38 was applied as the basis of judgment to preliminarily identify the presence of toxins in positive samples.

In this study, the confirmatory and quantitative methods of d-SPE-LC/MS/MS was established after fluorescence detection, as well as the linear equation, LOD, LOQ and recovery rate were obtained (Table 1 ).

For mushroom sample, the concentration range was 2∼100 μg/kg with good linear equation (R 2 = 0.9900∼0.9988), as well as LOD was 0.5 μg/kg and LOQ was 2.0 μg/kg. Similarly, blank urine samples were also with good linearity in the range of 0.5∼100 μg/L, R2 > 0.9910, LOD and LOQ were 0.2 and 0.5 μg/L, respectively. Recovery studies were performed on three different concentrations of spiked blank samples (2.0, 50.0 and 100.0 μg/L for mushrooms and 1.0, 10.0 and 100.0 μg/L for urine) to evaluate precision and accuracy. As shown in Table 2 , the intra-day and inter-day recovery rates of mushroom samples ranged from 81.3% to 104.9%, and the RSD values ranged from 0.9% to 7.7%. The intra-day and inter-day recovery rates of urine samples ranged from 72.7% to 110.1%, and the RSD values ranged from 1.1% to 6.1%. These quality control data meet the requirements of GB and can be used for the analysis of toxins in mushrooms and urine. The results showed that COF@VBC@BA-CDs had good adsorption efficiency for amatoxins and phallotoxins. Therefore, the designed d-SPE-LC/MS/MS method was suitable for qualitative and quantitative of toxins in mushroom and urine samples.

In the following experiment, forty mushroom samples were briefly treated and dispersed into COF@VBC@BA-CDs suspension to determine F value. Under the above optimized conditions, F/F0 value was obtained (Table S4). It can be seen that the fluorescence response of mushroom samples serial number (1–39) was poor. The fluorescence quenching of the mushroom sample with serial number (40) was more obvious. Its F/F0 was lower than the base value of F/F0 (0.38), indicating the presence of one positive sample in 40 test mushroom samples. In addition, it is important to analyze the toxins in the urine of mushroom poisoning patients. So, we also studied four urine samples of poisoned people from medical institutions, which were approved for medical ethics. All collected samples were initially screened using FLD, as well as the results showed that there was one positive sample in the four urine samples.

Subsequently, d-SPE-LC-MS/MS was applied for next quantitative analysis. Fig. 3 A-B showed the chromatogram of mushroom and urine samples respectively. The extraction of ion peaks were obtained and compared with standard solution (retention time and ion abundance ratio) using LC-MS/MS analysis. For positive mushroom samples, α-AMA and β-AMA were detected, as well as the contents were 70.5 and 161.0 μg/kg, respectively. For positive urine samples, only α-AMA (3.2 μg/L) was detected. In addition, we also randomly tested negative samples, in which none of the pending toxins were detected. Therefore, the established method can be used for rapid FLD screening and LC-MS/MS quantitative analysis of amatoxins and phallotoxins.

In this study, COF and BA-CDs were combined to design a mixed mode adsorbent with boric acid group (COF@VBC@BA-CDs). The adsorbent matched the structure of the amatoxins and phallotoxins, as well as had high adsorption capacity and good selectivity. Firstly, the luminescence characteristic of BA-CDs was used to select positive samples from a large number of samples using FLD, which greatly saved time and material resources. Then, the combination of targets and adsorbents in positive samples were collected and eluted for next LC-MS/MS detection. Under optimal conditions, this designed method had good sensitivity, wide linear range, satisfactory accuracy and precision for the analysis of six amatoxins and phallotoxins. The established analytical method provides a noval idea for the determination of toxins in practical samples and other related research work, as well as has good application value.

Yin-Hai Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Fei Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wei-Long Zhao: Software, Formal analysis. Xiao-Fan Tang: Validation, Investigation. Feng Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Chun-Miao Bo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22364001 and 22064013), Nature Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2023AAC03259), Science and Technology Support Program of Yinchuan, Ningxia (No. 2024SF001), Youth Talent Cultivation Project of North Minzu University (No. 2021KYQD30), University level key research project of North Minzu University (No. 2023ZRLG19).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2024.465515.
